title: Writing by IF - Medium
description: Trust is the new experience, and it’s for all of us to design. Follow for blog posts on design, technology and trust by the team at IF. - Medium

items.title: Frictionless is a risk
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: There’s a strong instinct to make AI-enabled services feel seamless, fast and invisible. That instinct comes from a good place. It doesn’t make sense to make a product unnecessarily complicated to use, and friction has become the enemy of good design. There’s a reason why Steve Krug’s classic book…

items.title: Just enough trust
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: Organisations generally want to gain people’s trust, and maximise this to the greatest extent possible, but what we’ve found during our decade working on this issue is that this is the wrong goal. A system people trust completely and without question is as dangerous as one they don’t trust at all.…

items.title: Trust is loaned, not owned
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: Many organisations talk about trust as if it’s a stable asset: something they build, own and occasionally repair if damage occurs. They measure it in surveys and assign it to the communications team to manage. But trust is not something that’s owned in this way. It’s loaned to you by the people who…

items.title: Publishing into the void
items.author: Gemma Lord
items.excerpt: Photo by Claudio Schwarz on Unsplash The missing feedback in the UK’s public data ecosystem Every time a council registers a planning application, a birth or death is recorded, or the environment agency logs a flood warning — that’s public sector information (PSI). It’s unglamorous, administrative,…

items.title: Ten years. Ten discoveries about trust.
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: In 2015, I gave a TEDx talk that nobody seemed very interested in. The dominant conversation in technology at the time was about open data: what governments should publish, what people could access. My talk was about the social contract. I wanted to know how relationships between people and…

items.title: Your LLM isn’t getting better. The services it uses are.
items.author: Gemma Lord
items.excerpt: We’re in a moment right now that design leaders should be excited about. Amidst a wave of AI explorations and pilots, organisations are starting to commit to an agentic future, and to confront a more fundamental question: what does it actually feel like to use products and services when AI is…

items.title: Leading in a relational world
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: If you’re leading a digital team, you’ve probably felt the frustration: the strategy’s solid, the data’s good, but progress still stalls. It’s rarely the technology. More often, it’s the relationships. I want to offer an idea. We’re still treating data and AI as things to control instead of…

items.title: 3 design patterns for consentful data sharing
items.author: Rob Bowman
items.excerpt: Consent is still treated as a one-off checkbox, while organisations’ use of data is constantly changing — including its use in training AI systems. This post introduces three new patterns to our design catalogue that reframe consent as something visible, revisitable, and meaningful. These patterns…

items.title: 2025: the year trust won
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: 2025 made one thing unmistakably clear: the work of trust can’t wait. As AI systems shape more of our services and decisions, organisations are realising they need more than velocity. They need confidence, transparency and feedback loops that keep them in control. This was the year IF scaled to…

items.title: Closing the AI adoption gap: lessons from Monzo, Experian and DeepMind
items.author: Sarah Gold
items.excerpt: A familiar pattern is emerging in organisations trying to adopt AI. Pilots appear everywhere, teams experiment, but very little of it turns into something people can actually use. Many leaders I speak to say roughly the same thing: “We know AI is important, but the value is always one step away.”…
